edges.quote: This bequest would prove to be most unwise, as later events would demonstrate.
edges.note: Aenys's coronation gift of Blackfyre and 'we shall rule together' pledge to Maegor
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: Gyldayn explicitly flags the bequest as feeding Maegor's later claim to sole rule, but Maegor's decision to act on it comes years later and is his own free choice (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: Within an hour of the king's death, she had mounted Vhagar and flown east across the narrow sea. When she returned, Prince Maegor was with her, on Balerion.
edges.note: Visenya fetches exiled Maegor from Pentos within an hour of Aenys's death
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the very next beat after Aenys's death, no decisional gap — Visenya acts within the hour.

edges.quote: “The Iron Throne will go to the man who has the strength to seize it," Maegor replied. Whereupon he decreed the immediate execution of the Grand Maester, taking off Gawen’s old grey head himself with a single swing of Blackfyre.
edges.note: Gawen's objection to Maegor's self-coronation gets him beheaded on the spot
edges.rationale: CAUSES: Maegor's own decree of execution is a direct real-causation response to Gawen's objection to the just-claimed throne, narrated as one continuous beat.

edges.quote: Prince Aegon was determined to claim his birthright.
edges.note: Being supplanted by his uncle drives Aegon's later rebellion
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: Aegon's rebellion is his own free decision to contest the usurpation, not an automatic consequence.

edges.quote: A hush fell over the hall at those words, for all present knew that title had hitherto belonged to Prince Maegor.
edges.note: Stripping Maegor of Prince of Dragonstone in favor of Aegon feeds Maegor's grievance
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: this public humiliation is cited by Gyldayn as souring the brothers' relationship and feeding Maegor's later 'strength to seize it' claim, mediated by Maegor's own choice years later.

edges.quote: At the feast afterward, King Aenys compounded his misjudgment by granting the title Prince of Dragonstone to his presumptive heir, Prince Aegon.
edges.note: At the wedding feast itself, Aenys grants Aegon the title that had belonged to Maegor
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'at the feast afterward' — the very next beat of the same occasion, no decisional gap.

edges.quote: The Faithful were listening. Less than a fortnight later, as Septon Murmison was crossing the city in his litter, a group of Poor Fellows came swarming from an alley and hacked him to pieces with their axes.
edges.note: Poor Fellows hack Murmison to pieces after he officiated the forbidden brother-sister wedding
edges.rationale: CAUSES: several intermediate steps (Murmison performing the rite, his expulsion from the Faith, the High Septon's 'King Abomination' denunciation) sit between the marriage and the killing, so CAUSES rather than TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: Three days before they were to sail, two Poor Fellows scaled the manse’s walls and broke into the king's bedchamber. Only the timely intervention of the Kingsguard saved Aenys from an ignoble death.
edges.note: Murmison's murder is followed directly by an assassination attempt on Aenys himself
edges.rationale: CAUSES: same wave of Faith Militant radicalization that killed Murmison escalates immediately to an attempt on the king's own life; the Kingsguard's intervention (a third party's action) is what prevents the outcome, so CAUSES (not TRIGGERS, since the manse fortification/relocation decision intervenes).

edges.quote: or suffer five years of exile. Prince Maegor chose exile.
edges.note: Aenys's ultimatum over the Alys Harroway marriage forces Maegor's choice of exile
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: Maegor's choice of exile over abandoning Alys is his own free decision (agency-collapse check) made in response to the marriage scandal.

edges.quote: But the revels died away when Maegor mounted Balerion and descended upon the Hill of Rhaenys, where seven hundred of the Warrior's Sons were at their morning prayers in the fortified sept.
edges.note: Recovered from his Trial of Seven wounds, Maegor burns the surviving Warrior's Sons at their fortified sept
edges.rationale: CAUSES: Maegor's dragonfire attack is the direct sequel to his survival of the trial (28 days of recovery under Tyanna's care sit between), a real multi-step causal chain rather than an instantaneous trigger.

edges.quote: Battle was joined first in the Reach, at the town of Stonebridge.
edges.note: The High Septon's call to arms after the sept-burning produces open battle at Stonebridge
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: the High Septon's decision to call 'true and pious children of the gods' to arms is a free decision by a third party mediating between the burning and the battle (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: King Maegor let the poisons fester for almost half a year, so engrossed was he in the building of his Red Keep. It was his mother who struck first.
edges.note: Victory at the Great Fork frees the crown's hand, but Maegor delays half a year before the burnings begin
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the military defeat of the Faith's field army is a precondition for the punitive burning campaign but does not force its timing or occurrence — Visenya's own decision ('it was his mother who struck first') completes the causation.

edges.quote: His Grace's long absence from King’s Landing had not gone unnoticed
edges.note: Maegor's extended stay in Oldtown after the Faith's submission leaves King's Landing unguarded
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Maegor's prolonged absence is an opportunity, not a compulsion — Aegon's own decision to seize the moment completes the causation, preserving agency.

edges.quote: Emerging at last from Casterly Rock, Aegon the Uncrowned and his wife, Rhaena, raced across the riverlands with a handful of companions and entered the city concealed beneath sacks of corn.
edges.note: The dragon-theft raid is the opening beat of Aegon's rebellion against Maegor
edges.rationale: Structural PART_OF: the raid to reclaim Quicksilver and Dreamfyre is narrated as the direct opening move of the wider rebellion that culminates at the Battle Beneath the Gods Eye.

edges.quote: Lady Jeyne of House Westerling had been married to Alyn Tarbeck, who had died with Prince Aegon in the Battle Beneath the Gods Eye.
edges.note: The battle's dead (Aegon, Alyn Tarbeck) create the pool of 'widows of proven fertility' Maegor marries three years later
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the battle's casualties are the precondition that makes Rhaena and Jeyne available as brides, but Maegor's decision to marry three widows at once is his own free choice.

edges.quote: Dragged from her bed, Queen Alys saw
edges.note: The monstrous stillbirth triggers Maegor's investigation, torture, and confessions that end in Alys's arrest and death
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the stillbirth sets off a direct, textually explicit chain (suspicion → Tyanna's list → torture → confessions → arrest) ending in Alys's execution; real causation through intermediate steps.

edges.quote: King Maegor himself departed King’s Landing, assembling a strong force of knights and men-at-arms and marching on Harrenhal to complete the destruction of House Harroway.
edges.note: Maegor personally leads the campaign that annihilates House Harroway immediately after Alys's execution
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the text frames the military campaign as a direct continuation ('to complete the destruction') of the same purge that killed Alys.

edges.quote: On the nights when the king was sharing a bed with Queen Ceryse or Queen Tyanna, Lucas Harroway sent men to his daughter's bed to get her with child.
edges.note: Lucas Harroway's standing as Hand of the King gives him the access and cover to arrange the scheme that produces the stillbirth
edges.rationale: ENABLES: his position as Hand is a precondition of opportunity, not a guarantee — the scheme itself is Lucas and Alys's own decision (stub wired).

edges.quote: Seeing Queen Ceryse return to court, old and bitter and childless, Alys Harroway had begun to fear that the same fate awaited her unless. she gave the king a son, so she had turned to her lord father, the Hand of the King.
edges.note: Ceryse's restoration to court (from the Oldtown reconciliation) drives Alys's fear-based decision to seek an heir by illicit means
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Ceryse's return is the motivating fear behind Alys's own decision to conspire with her father, a human decision mediates so MOTIVATES not CAUSES.

edges.quote: In the aftermath of the wedding, Maegor declared Rhaena’s daughter Aerea his lawful heir
edges.note: Maegor names Rhaena's daughter Aerea his heir in the immediate aftermath of the triple wedding
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'in the aftermath of the wedding' — the decree is the very next beat of the same occasion.

edges.quote: His nephew Jaehaerys, the rightful heir by all the laws of the Seven Kingdoms, was expressly disinherited in the same decree.
edges.note: Jaehaerys's disinheritance is a core grievance behind Lord Rogar's later proclamation of him as true king
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the uprising is a deliberate political act by Rogar Baratheon and Jaehaerys, not an automatic consequence of the decree.

edges.quote: But even in death, Tyanna of the Tower had her revenge, for it came to pass just as she had promised.
edges.note: Tyanna's promised poisoning of Elinor's child comes to pass after Maegor kills her
edges.rationale: CAUSES, but disputed: Gyldayn frames this as revenge 'even in death,' an inherently uncertain/folkloric causal claim (poison administered earlier vs. coincidence); no living decision mediates the stillbirth itself, so CAUSES rather than MOTIVATES, but tier-2 + disputed given the supernatural framing.

edges.quote: No man in the Seven Kingdoms could doubt that the king was accursed now. What followers still remained to him began to melt away, evaporating like dew in the morning sun.
edges.note: The third monstrous stillbirth convinces lords Maegor is doomed, and they begin abandoning him for Jaehaerys
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the perception that the king is 'accursed' is the psychological opening the defecting lords use to justify rebellion, a human political decision mediates.

edges.quote: When word reached the Red Keep that Jaehaerys was gathering his forces at Storm's End, Rhaena Targaryen mounted Dreamfyre and flew to join him, abandoning the uncle she had been forced to wed.
edges.note: Rhaena flies to join Jaehaerys the moment word of his muster at Storm's End reaches her
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: her flight is the immediate next beat upon hearing the news, no other decision intervenes (stub wired).

edges.quote: Queen Elinor found him still upon the Iron Throne, pale and dead, his robes soaked through with blood.
edges.note: Maegor is found dead on the Iron Throne the same night his war council collapses under the mass defections
edges.rationale: CAUSES, but disputed: the uprising's collapse of Maegor's support (down to four thousand men, Lord Hayford urging abdication) is the plain causal frame Gyldayn gives, but the literal mechanism of death is explicitly disputed in-text (murder by the throne's blades, Elinor, the Kingsguard, or suicide), so tier-2 + disputed.

edges.quote: In their place, he decreed, a great stone “stable for dragons” would be erected, a lair worthy of Balerion, Vhagar, and their get. Thus commenced the building of the Dragonpit.
edges.note: The ashes and ruins of the burned Sept of Remembrance are cleared to make way for the Dragonpit
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the earlier burning is the physical precondition (the ruin that gets cleared) for the Dragonpit's construction years later, but Maegor's decision to build there is his own choice.

edges.quote: In the chaos that had followed the discovery of Maegor’s body, Lord Rosby drank a cup of hemlock to join his king in death.
edges.note: Rosby kills himself in the chaos immediately following the discovery of Maegor's body
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Rosby's suicide is his own free decision made in direct response to his king's death; target is the decision-event itself (agency-preserving).

edges.quote: Moon's murder removed the last major obstacle to the accession of Jaehaerys Targaryen to the Iron Throne
edges.note: Septon Moon's assassination removes the last obstacle blocking Jaehaerys's safe journey to be crowned at Oldtown (seed row 11)
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: Gyldayn's own word is 'obstacle removed' — the coronation still requires Jaehaerys's court to travel to Oldtown and the High Septon to perform the rite, decisional steps by multiple parties.

edges.quote: After seizing the septon’s body from Rob the Starvling, this “learned” fool strapped it atop a destrier, naked, bloody, and rotting, to storm the gates of Oldtown.
edges.note: Lorcas seizes Moon's corpse and mounts a doomed attack on Oldtown in the immediate aftermath of the assassination
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: Lorcas's attack follows directly from the power vacuum and corpse-dispute created by Moon's murder, the next beat in the same collapse (stub wired).

edges.quote: it was widely believed that someone—Queen Rhaena herself, mayhaps, or her mother, Queen Alyssa—had used the occasion of the king's coronation to switch the twins.
edges.note: The gathering of the whole family at Jaehaerys's coronation is the occasion used to secretly swap Aerea and Rhaella
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the coronation creates the opportunity (both twins together, one returning to King's Landing) but does not compel the switch, which is Rhaena/Alyssa's own alleged decision; tier-2 + disputed since the text says 'nothing was ever proved.'

new_nodes.name: Aenys bequeaths Blackfyre to Maegor
new_nodes.identity: At his own coronation on Dragonstone in 37 AC, newly-crowned King Aenys I tells his half-brother Maegor 'we shall rule this realm together, you and I' and gifts him their father's Valyrian steel blade Blackfyre — a bequest Gyldayn explicitly calls unwise, since it fed Maegor's later claim that the throne belonged to whoever had the strength to seize it.

new_nodes.name: Death of Aenys I Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: Worn down by the Faith Militant's rebellion against his rule, King Aenys I collapses and dies at Dragonstone three days after learning that Poor Fellows have surrounded his son and daughter at Crakehall Castle, leaving the throne vacant and his half-brother Maegor free to claim it.

new_nodes.name: Maegor claims the Iron Throne
new_nodes.identity: Within hours of Aenys's death, Dowager Queen Visenya fetches Maegor from exile in Pentos; he lands on Dragonstone and has her crown him with Aegon the Conqueror's iron crown, bypassing Aenys's son and heir Aegon and beheading Grand Maester Gawen for objecting.

new_nodes.name: Death of Maegor I Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: With his support collapsed to a handful of crownland lords and his nephew Jaehaerys mustering an army at Storm's End, Maegor I Targaryen is found dead upon the Iron Throne at dawn, his arms slashed on the throne's blades and a blade through his neck; murder, suicide, and death-by-throne are all argued by the maesters, and he leaves no heir of his body.

flags: row-79 slug_candidates trystane-mounts-iron-throne / siege-of-maegors-holdfast / judgment-of-princess-saera-before-the-iron-throne / execution-of-rego-drazs-killers / battle-at-the-maegors-holdfast-drawbridge checked and rejected: siege-of-maegors-holdfast and battle-at-the-maegors-holdfast-drawbridge are Dance-of-the-Dragons-era (135 AC) nodes, not 42 AC Maegor's-usurpation nodes; the other three are unrelated wrong-era token-overlap false positives. Row 79 wired instead via new node maegor-claims-the-iron-throne -> execution-of-grand-maester-gawen (B-E03).

No pre-existing node for 'death of Aenys' or 'Maegor's own death' or 'Maegor claims the throne' — minted death-of-aenys-i-targaryen, maegor-claims-the-iron-throne, death-of-maegor-i-targaryen (plus aenys-bequeaths-blackfyre-to-maegor for row 67) as the missing marquee anchors; existing funeral-of-king-aenys node was left untouched (it specifically covers the pyre-lighting, not the death itself).

Roster items harren-the-reds-rebellion and invasion-of-the-iron-islands (Lodos suppression) were reviewed: both are contemporaneous with Aenys's reign but the text frames them as parallel 'false king' rebellions explicitly CONTRASTED with (not causally linked to) the Faith Militant uprising that follows; no clean anchor to wire them without minting additional nodes for peripheral beats (e.g. Maegor's first Hand appointment after Harren's death), so left unwired this session rather than force a weak edge.

The Jeyne Westerling stillbirth (Tyanna's confession also names it, fab-sons-of-the-dragon-05-p03:205) has no dedicated node in the graph; only Alys Harroway's and Elinor Costayne's stillbirths do. Worth a future stub mint if the causal spine revisits Maegor's remarriage arc.

harvest.note: Rhaena to Poor Fellows pelting her with dirt: “You are fearless when facing a girl on a horse, I see. The next time I come, I will be on a dragon. Throw dirt on me then, I pray you.” Strong homeless characterization quote.
Rhaena on hearing of Aegon's death at the Battle Beneath the Gods Eye: “I do not have the time for tears.” Homeless quote, strong characterization.

Lord Lucas Harroway refuses to admit Aegon and Rhaena's wedding progress to Harrenhal unless they acknowledge Alys as Maegor's lawful wife; they spend 'a cold wet night in tents beneath the towering walls of Black Harren's mighty castle' — hospitality-refusal beat.
Aegon (son of Aenys) described as 'lean and handsome and growing taller every year... the very image of his grandsire at the same age' — physical description tying Aegon to Aegon the Conqueror.

Grand Maester Benifer on young Aerea: 'She is frightened of horses, dogs, boys with loud voices, men with beards, and dancing, and she is terrified of dragons' — vivid child-character description, useful for the switching-of-the-twins node.
